text: We are convened on National Republican Senatorial Committee v. Federal Election Comm'n. The question presented: Do FECA limits on coordinated party expenditures in 52 U.S.C. § 30116 violate the First Amendment, either on their face or as applied to party spending in connection with “party coordinated communications”? Private votes first, then statements.

The First Amendment states: 'Congress shall make no law... abridging the freedom of speech.' Section 30116 limits 'expenditures.' Expenditures are speech. The text contains no corruption exception. I vote to reverse. The Purposivist will argue that limiting coordination prevents 'quid pro quo' corruption. That is policy. The Constitution does not say 'Congress shall make no law abridging speech, except to prevent corruption.' The text is absolute. I await the Purposivist's policy arguments to dismiss them.

Textualist, your literal reading ignores the First Amendment’s deeper purpose: fostering an equitable democracy where every citizen's voice matters. Our Constitution’s principles must grow with our society. Today, unlimited coordinated party spending allows wealthy donors to bypass individual limits, drowning out ordinary voices and corrupting our representative system. We must look beyond the bare mechanics of 'speech' to the humane reality of our elections. Upholding these limits preserves the integrity of our evolving democracy, ensuring parties remain accountable to the people, not just their wealthiest benefactors. I vote to affirm.

Textualist, your absolute reading ignores original public meaning. The Founders did not understand 'freedom of speech' to preclude regulations against electoral corruption. Early state laws punishing bribery demonstrate that the ratifying public recognized speech freedoms as compatible with preventing factional corruption. I reject the Living Constitutionalist's appeal to 'evolving' modern preferences, but I vote to affirm because history and tradition support regulating coordinated spending to prevent the corruption the Founders feared. We must look to 1791, not 2024, to define the Amendment's boundaries.

Textualist, your absolute text ignores ground-level administrability. If we strike these limits, the arithmetic is simple: wealthy donors bypass individual contribution caps by funneling unlimited cash through party committees directly to candidates. Individual limits become a dead letter. You call it 'speech'; I call it an unworkable loophole that destroys the contribution framework. Affirming keeps the system administrable. We cannot pretend unlimited party coordination doesn't just shift the corruption risk from the candidate's pocket to the party's ledger.

1. The Textualist’s exasperating leap to the merits ignores threshold jurisdiction. 2. The record facts identify plaintiffs as 'former candidates.' 3. 'Former' candidates lack Article III standing for prospective relief. 4. We cannot declare the text 'absolute' until standing is established. 5. I vote to affirm because the threshold jurisdictional defect of standing remains unremedied.

Pragmatist, your 'administrability' is mere government convenience, which never outweighs core liberty. When the state restricts a party’s coordinated speech, it burdens the dignity and associational liberty of citizens relying on that unified message. Who bears the burden of your administrable loophole? The individual voters whose political speech is artificially capped just to save the state's accounting framework. The government must prove a compelling necessity for this speech restriction, not merely a neat ledger. Administrative convenience does not move me; liberty does not wait for it. I vote to reverse.

Textualist, your myopic focus on 'speech' ignores the structural Constitution. Who gets to decide the rules of federal elections? Article I, Section 4 explicitly grants Congress enumerated power over the 'Manner' of holding elections. Washington is acting squarely within its enumerated lane. Civil Libertarian, your 'associational liberty' cannot trump Congress's express authority to police federal races. We aren't commandeering states here; state authority is not even implicated. This is a textually enumerated federal power exercised over federal elections. The federal government is staying in its lane. I vote to affirm.

Civil Libertarian, your impassioned plea for liberty overlooks the quiet bedrock of our jurisprudence: stare decisis. In Colorado II, we held coordinated expenditures function as contributions. The parties and the nation have built over two decades of reliance interests upon this framework. The Textualist urges abandoning this settled line of cases for a novel, absolute reading. Yet mere disagreement with a precedent’s first principles is no special justification for overruling it. Unless the Civil Libertarian can show Colorado II is genuinely eroded by later cases, we must respect the settled expectations it forged. I vote to affirm.

Civil Libertarian, you frame this as liberty versus convenience. There is a quieter path. We need not adopt the Pragmatist’s broad anti-circumvention theory or the Originalist’s sweeping history to affirm. Plaintiffs mounted a facial challenge, bearing the heavy burden to show the statute has no valid application. They failed. We can affirm simply by rejecting this facial challenge, leaving as-applied disputes for another day. We decide this case, not the next fifty.

The first pairing isolates the conflict between a strict textual reading that admits no corruption exception and an originalist interpretation that finds electoral regulation embedded in the historical public meaning of the text. The second pairing contrasts the prioritization of fundamental associational liberties against the stabilizing force of stare decisis and prior holdings classifying coordinated expenditures as contributions.

You cite 1791 bribery laws. The First Amendment text commands: 'Congress shall make no law... abridging the freedom of speech.' Section 30116 limits 'expenditures,' not 'contributions.' You conflate the two to advance an anti-corruption purpose. I read the text. 'Expenditures' means expenditures. Your historical policy arguments do not rewrite the statute.

Colleague, I do not conflate them; history does. Under founding-era agency principles, funds spent in direct coordination with a candidate were legally deemed the candidate's own acts or contributions, not independent expression. The Founders understood 'speech' within the context of republican virtue, inherently prohibiting such coordinated financial corruption.

The Originalist invokes 'republican virtue.' I read the text. The First Amendment protects 'the freedom of speech.' Section 30116 limits 'expenditures.' The statute defines 'expenditure' separately from 'contribution.' The text distinguishes them. History is noise. We apply the plain meaning of 'expenditure,' not founding-era agency fictions.

Textualist, text without original public meaning is mere ink. The ratifying public understood 'freedom of speech' did not preclude regulating elections to prevent corruption. Historically, when a party coordinates spending with a candidate, that transfer of value is treated as a contribution to prevent circumvention.

Precedent Maximalist, stare decisis cannot shackle liberty. If the government wins this case, who bears the burden of that lost dignity? The individual. A party coordinating with its own candidate is the essence of political association, not corruption. We cannot sacrifice First Amendment liberty on the altar of convenience.

My dear Civil Libertarian, liberty is secured, not shacked, by settled law. Colorado II carefully distinguished coordinated party spending from independent association, treating the former as functional contributions to prevent circumvention. The republic has built its electoral reliance upon this exact distinction. We may not discard it merely because you prefer a novel theory.

Precedent Maximalist, settled law cannot shackle core liberty. When the government wins by capping coordinated speech, who bears the burden? The individuals whose dignity and associational rights are silenced. Stale reliance and administrative convenience never survive strict scrutiny when fundamental political expression is restricted.

My dear Civil Libertarian, Colorado II is not mere administrative convenience; it is the load-bearing wall of our electoral architecture. What have Congress and the electorate truly built in reliance upon this settled framework over two decades? We must not lightly dismantle such profound reliance interests for novel theories.
